desc: Formerly Sachem - a ubiquitous later summer Skipper. This abundant Skipper is worth learning carefully as it has a lot of variation and you will see it more frequently then any other Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Look for Large black patch on forewing.  Female with two pale spots on Forewing.
fm.Underside: Female with pale crecent, Male paler with dark patch on Hindwing
fm.Habitat: Backyards, fields and many other open areas

desc: This is probably the most familiar butterfly.  Beautiful orange wings with black veins and white spots.

fm.Upperside: Orange with black veins and white spots
fm.Underside: Orange with black veins
fm.Habitat: Fields and open areas. Can be seen migrating in Fall.

desc: Distinctive, yellow and black striped butterfly that sometimes shows a dark morph that could be confused with Black Swallowtail.

fm.Upperside: Yellow wings with black border and internal stripes.  Can show black morph.
fm.Underside: Pale yellow with black border and bluish submarginal hindwing band
fm.Host Plants: Wild Black Cherry and Tulip Tree

desc: While Black is our most common black swallowtail, Eastern Tiger Swallow tail frequently shows a dark morph and Spicebrush Swallowtail, seen briefly can cause identification issues.  Black and Tiger have different shapes which may be helpful but a careful examination of wing pattern, especially underwing pattern, might be key to unlocking a dark Swallowtail's true identity.

fm.Wingspan: 1.5 in, smaller than Eastern Tiger Swallowtail
fm.Upperside: Forewing distinctive with yellow spotting on Male and Females.
fm.Underside: lacks orange spots on forewing. Hindwing with orange and blue spots.

desc: A ubiquitous butterfly found in many habitats. Underwings can be quite colorful but often seen in flight when white wings are obvious.

fm.Upperside: White with three black spots
fm.Underside: White, not heavily marked.  Can look quite yellowish.
fm.Habitat: Open fields, gardens and disturbed areas

desc: A small grass Skipper with dramatic sexual dimorphism. At times this species is abundant.  Worth spending time with it when available as getting to know its sexual and individual variation inside-out may help alert you to other species.  Similar to Hobomock Skipper but with distinctive underwing patterns.

fm.Upperside: Male hindwing with bright yellow-orange bordered with black.  Female with disinctive white spots on forewing.
fm.Underside: Male yellow-orange circling dark patch on hindwing.  Female darker with pale edge to hindwing.
fm.Habitat: Edge of woodlands and open areas

desc: A very small skipper with distinctive underwing pattern.

fm.Upperside: Not especially distinctive, but look to hindwing with long pale central patch.
fm.Underside: Variable but distinctive. Pale submarginal border with central cell much longer than others.
fm.Habitat: Open areas with nectar, various habitats.

desc: A striking black and blue butterfly similar in appearance to Pipevine Swallowtail. Also know as Red-spotted Purple.

fm.Upperside: Black with iridescent blue. Orange spots on forewing.
fm.Underside: White and black margined wtih orange spots submarginally on forewing and hindwing.

desc: A small blue butterfly that flys low to the ground with distinctive tail-like extensions on the hindwings.

fm.Upperside: Blue with small tails on hindwing and orange spots.
fm.Underside: blue-gray with orange spot on hindwing near tails.

desc: A medium sized spreadwing skipper with distinctive silver band on the hindwing and large orange stripe on forewing.  Only Hoary Edge a much less common butterfly should pose any real identification issues.  Silver-spotteds are conspiculous and un-shy.

fm.Upperside: Brownish or tawny. Forewing shape somewhat triangular
fm.Underside: White or white-gold bandlike spot on hindwing. Visible in flight.
fm.Habitat: Various open habitats and woodland edges.

desc: A distinctive black butterfly with red bands and white spots.

fm.Upperside: Black with stunning red bands on forewing and hindwing
fm.Underside: similar to Ladies butterflies

desc: An abundant little butterfly with a spreadwing behavior that can be numerous in open areas.

fm.Upperside: mosaic orange & black wings with strong blackish border
fm.Underside: Not often seen, pale or warmly golden
fm.Habitat: Open fields, whether meadows or backyards

desc: A prolific flyer, often seen on migration.  Easily identifiable in flight with it's pale forewing bars.

fm.Upperside: Pale bar on forewing and several 'eyes' around margin of FW and HW.
fm.Underside: More cryptic, pale FW bar still visible

desc: Very similar to Black Swallowtail but lacks yellow markings.  Could also be confused with Red Spotted Admiral or Pipevine Swallowtail which is much less common in our area.

fm.Upperside: Black with bluish-white markings especially in hindwing
fm.Underside: Two rows of orange dots on hindwing, no yellow.

desc: A fairly unique butterfly. From above looks similar to other Fritillarys, with brownish-orange and pale wings with black cell borders and spotting.  From below it is quite different from our other Fritillarys.

fm.Upperside: Dramatic pattern of dull and pale orange bordered by black, some black spots.
fm.Underside: Unlike other Fritillary butterflies, more cryptic with pale bar on HW

desc: Very similar to Question Mark and often and eespecially in flight may be indistinquishable.  Important to know the sex and to wait for it to land in order to examin spotting on forewing.

fm.Wingspan: Smaller than American Lady and Question Mark
fm.Upperside: Orangish with fewer black spots in FW. HW can be dark or bright orange (brood specific)
fm.Underside: Cryptic patern with pale comma.

desc: Probably the most common Hairstreak in the county and worth knowing well.  It has more orange on it's underwing than most Hairstreaks and moves North in late Summer when it becomes more common.

fm.Upperside: Bright blue.
fm.Underside: A continuous orange-red band across HW and FW
fm.Habitat: Wood edges, brushy fields and even backyards.

desc: Very similar to Clouded Sulphur but with warmer orange coloring. Females may be indistinguishable.

fm.Upperside: Only seen in flight, has yellow-orange coloration and black edging.
fm.Underside: Males are more warmly colored, less of a blackish hue to leading edge.

desc: Basically the same as Spring Azure but flies later in the year.  It has no orange spotting and lacks tails.

fm.Wingspan: Bigger than an Eastern Tailed Blue.
fm.Upperside: Blue with paler edging.  No tails, no red marks.
fm.Underside: Pale with neat black markings
fm.Habitat: Fields, backyards and open wooded areas.

desc: A very close relative of the Painted Lady.  This pair, despite striking colors can pose identification issues if not seen well. American is the more deeply orange of the pair, especially in the leading edge of the FW.  It also has less black in the interior of the FW.

fm.Upperside: Orangish and black with several white spots on edge of forewing
fm.Underside: Two 'eyes' on hindwing distinctive.

desc: A ligth brown, understated and distinctive Butterfly with unique flight style. Can be quite common in appropriate habitat or the right time of year.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown with four eyes.
fm.Underside: Gray-brown with two eyes on FW and HW.
fm.Habitat: Grassy areas next to woods and forest.

desc: One of the larger and palest tailed Hairstreaks

fm.Upperside: Grayish-blue with one orange spot in HW.
fm.Underside: Has narrow black & white bar across HW and FW. One to two large orange spots in HW.

desc: Tiny orangish grass skipper

fm.Upperside: Dark blackish wings
fm.Underside: Brightly orange with rounded tips
fm.Habitat: Wet grassy areas and marshes, occasionally backyards.

desc: Large orange butterfly with conspicuous white spotting below.

fm.Upperside: Richly patterned Forewing contrasts with light patterning on HW.
fm.Underside: Hindwing wtih pale white spots and wide creamy postmedian band

desc: Very similar to Eastern Comma. Has two broods that are dimorphic.

fm.Wingspan: Larger than American Lady, bigger than E. Comma
fm.Upperside: Orangish forwing has additional black dots.  Hindwing can be orange or black with pale edging
fm.Underside: Cryptically mottled with a pale question mark.

desc: A long living, distinctive looking butterfly.

fm.Upperside: Blackish with bold yellow trailing edge and blue submarginal spots.
fm.Underside: Dark brownish/black-gray with paler border.

desc: Very similar to American Lady, less orange and with more 'eyes' below on HW. Leading edge can have additional pale pink or almost white spot not seen in American lady.

fm.Upperside: Less orange compared to American Lady, pinkish with more black in forewing
fm.Underside: at least four 'eye' spots in sub marginal section of hindwing distinctive.

desc: A large very dark spreadwing Skipper with several lookalikes.

fm.Upperside: Very dark, male with small pale spot near FW Cell end. Female appears more mottled.
fm.Underside: Very dark but lacks pale spots around top of Hindwing
fm.Habitat: Woods with Oak and adjacent open areas.

desc: A small very dark spreadwing Skipper with mottled patterns.

fm.Upperside: Has a patch of four white fingers on outer portion of forewing.
fm.Underside: Very dark with a few white or light spots

desc: The more showy of our two Emperors.

fm.Upperside: White spotting in Forewing, hindwing lighter with black spots.
fm.Underside: Cryptic but vibrant with many eyes along hindwing postmedian

desc: Muted pale gray-brown appearance.

fm.Upperside: Pale gray-brown, typically lacking eye-spots in FW
fm.Underside: Irrigular line of eyes in FW, straight lines on innner HW.
fm.Habitat: Wet woods and adjacent open areas.

desc: A unique and unmistakable butterfly. Unlike many butterflies with carnivorous caterpillars - they eat Wooly Affids!

fm.Upperside: Orange with blotchy black on the forewing
fm.Underside: Patches of various shades of Orange with small pale white bubbles.

desc: Basically identical to other Azures, but larger.  This one should be identified with caution.  Seeing it alongside a Summer Azure might be a good first step, or laying eggs on it's host plant. Of the ~30 records in iNaturalist, 29 are of catapillars and only one is of an adult butterfly.  ID we care!

fm.Upperside: Blue
fm.Underside: Pale blue with neat black markings might be paler than Summer Azure

desc: A large, plain and colorful Sulphur.

fm.Wingspan: One third larger than Small White
fm.Upperside: Only seen in flight.
fm.Underside: Males lack most markings, females with some pink on FW and white spots on HW.

desc: Very similar to Orange Sulphur but lacks orangish color.

fm.Upperside: Only seen in flight, lacks orange typically.
fm.Underside: Blackish border to leading edge of FW and HW.

desc: A dark Hespiria Skipper with distinctive FW spot.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with a large square or rectangular pale spot. White on antenae
fm.Underside: Dark brown with row of postmedial dots

desc: A distinctive fall Grass Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Orangish
fm.Underside: Orangish with black dots on Hindwing.

desc: A dull, small Grass Skipper. It really lacks a lof of the strong field marks our other Skippers display.

fm.Upperside: Dull brownish
fm.Underside: Yellowish-brown with prominent viens.

desc: A delightful spreadwing Skipper.  This black and white beauty can't really be confused with anything else in our area.  The only surprise is the habitat you might find it in as it thrives in some of the most "disturbed" areas.

fm.Upperside: Beautiful White with black checkerboard, bluish body
fm.Underside: unlikely to see much of it.

desc: Dark, mostly unmarked Hesperia Skipper

fm.Upperside: Males dark with black stigma.  Femailes Dark with at least two small spots.
fm.Underside: Brownish and unmarked

desc: A massive butterfly with distinct upperwing pattern. It's a slow flapper, almost gliding around but can move quickly enough.

fm.Upperside: Almost black with Yellow Bars forming X's in Forewing.
fm.Underside: Creamy yellow with blue median band
fm.Habitat: Open woodland, adjacent fields and hilltops.

desc: Very similar to Horace's and should be identified with care.

fm.Upperside: Males are almost entirely black with some mottling on outter edge. Females more black than Horace.
fm.Underside: Pale edged with two pale spots on top of hindwing.
fm.Habitat: Woods with Oak and adjacent open areas.

desc: A stunning dark blue Swallowtail. A few other Swallowtails or even a Red-spotted Admiral could be mistakenly ID as Pipevine but this species is unique.  Get a good look at one and it's easy to appreciate it's subtle shape and color differences.

fm.Upperside: Dark with darkblue Hindwing.
fm.Underside: Single band of orange spots on irridecent blue.

desc: A distinctive, tiny elephantine nosed butterfly. Snouts like to move around but aren't really fast, they are quick but have a certain floppyness to their flight.  Plus you can often spot the nose even in flight.

fm.Wingspan: Small, a bit bigger than a Pearl Crecent
fm.Upperside: Brown and Orange with white marks at tip of pointed Forewing.
fm.Underside: Hindwing cryptic, Forewing with splotch of orange
fm.Habitat: Woods, brushy areas and adjacent open areas.

desc: Like Hairstreaks but with vivid orange blocks on wing.

fm.Upperside: Forewing with broad orange block spotted with black, HW graish with orange band.
fm.Underside: Paler mirror of above wing appearance, black spotting more prominent.

desc: A beautiful Hairstreak to be enjoyed if seen.  Other Hairstreaks can alsoo show partial M-like markings on their hindwing.  Look also for orange limited to a single spot and a small white bar on the interior leading edge of the hindwing.

fm.Upperside: Bright blue.
fm.Underside: HW distinctive with single white bar internally near leading edge.  Distinctive white M near trailing edge and orange spot further from margin than other Hairstreaks.
fm.Habitat: Brushy fields, hilltops near Oak woodlands.

desc: A dull and featureless Hairstreak. Pale lines on underside are limited and fewer in number compared with rarer Striped Hairstreak.

fm.Upperside: Grayish
fm.Underside: Variable and muted.  Postmedian band has white markings only on outer edge.
fm.Habitat: Open fields with nectar flowers, near oak woodlands.

desc: A late season visitor, similar to Orange Sulphur but with black antenae.

fm.Upperside: Orange with black border, seen in flight.
fm.Underside: Either yellow-ish orange or dull tanish-red with narrow dark wash diagonally.

desc: A dully colored Hespiria Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Males dull on HW, FW with orange patch limited to leading edge, has black stigma.  Females with some pale spots and limited orange.
fm.Underside: Dull HW contrasts with brighter FW that has some orange.  Males often showing faint or no spots.
fm.Habitat: Any open area near nectar sources.

desc: Virtualy identical to Summer Azure but flies in the spring.

fm.Upperside: Blue
fm.Underside: Pale blue with neat black markings, margin maybe muddier
fm.Host Plants: Buds & Flowers of Dogwoods, Viburnums, New Jersey Tea

desc: A gorgeous emerald colored haristreak

fm.Upperside: Not often seen
fm.Underside: It's a stunning green and brown with white.

desc: Similar to much more common Zabulon Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Male orange with bold black border, black cell end bars.  Female darker with pale cell spot.
fm.Underside: Big brown splotch at base of wing with broad pale band centrally.  Lacks pale HW apex.

desc: A declining species(?) that is beautifuly colored.

fm.Upperside: Mostly black with white spots and orange border.
fm.Underside: White and black, orange border and orange interior spots.
fm.Habitat: Wet grassy areas and marshes with White Turtlehead, occasionally fields.

desc: Superficially shares characters with several disparatly related butterflies.

fm.Upperside: Orange and black forewing, hindwing mostly. dark
fm.Underside: Cryptic, lacking prominent eyes, some dark bands.
fm.Habitat: Near Hackberries, but can be see in a variety of habitat.

desc: A small orange Hesperia Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Forewing orange with black cell end bar and black veining.
fm.Underside: Unmarked yellow-orange.
fm.Habitat: Brushy fields with sedges, wet fields.

desc: A small Frittilary.

fm.Upperside: Orange and black, lacking black borders.
fm.Underside: Smudy and indistict.

desc: Smaller and not closely related to similar looking Monarch.

fm.Upperside: Very Orange, look for rounded band through Hindwing.
fm.Underside: Similar to Monarch but note crecents in margin and round black line through HW.
fm.Habitat: Open areas, near water and Willows.

desc: A small early season show stopper.

fm.Upperside: White with orange Forewing tips. Female retains shape but not the orange.
fm.Underside: Hindwing especially marbled, otherwise white.

desc: A large dull colored Hesperia Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Dark with several large orange spots
fm.Underside: Dull orange brown, maybe with some paler spots.
fm.Habitat: Irrupts(?) into our area, look for at nectar flowers.

desc: A tailless brownish-blue Hairstreak with striking HW.

fm.Upperside: Brownish grayish blue
fm.Underside: FW pale with some black dots, HW with striking row of coral spots near margin.

desc: A dull colored Hesperia Skipper best examined closely.

fm.Upperside: Both sexes have rectangular pale spot on FW. Male with two part stigma.
fm.Underside: Light brownish sometimes with purple sheen. HW with spots sort of in shape of #3.

desc: A small dark spreadwinged Skipper.

fm.Upperside: Dark with white spots on head and wings.
fm.Underside: Not often seen.

desc: A late summer visitor, distinctive.

fm.Upperside: Dark with translucent pale rounded comma shape in FW
fm.Underside: Long narrow wing shape, occasionally with faint purplish sheen. Abdomen streaked.

desc: A large dark skipper with limited markings.

fm.Upperside: Very dark with pale margin and limited white spots in Forewing
fm.Underside: Very dark, somewhat splotchy
fm.Habitat: Open areas, both disturbed and meadow.

desc: A dull Hesperia Skipper similar to Tawny-edge

fm.Upperside: Male with more orange than Tawny-edge, orange on both sides of stigma. Female with squarish pale spot near cell and less orange.
fm.Underside: Similar to Tawny-edge but much less contrast between HW and FW leading edge.

desc: Our largest Satyr, distinctive.

fm.Upperside: Only seen in flight. Brownish
fm.Underside: Forewing has distinctive pale-yellowish patch with two eyes.

desc: A Rockstar Skipper who visits from the South.  Distinctive coloration and shape.

fm.Upperside: Long irridecent blueish tails on HW
fm.Underside: Dark banding and pale edging

desc: A rare hairstreak resembling White-M and Gray Hairstreaks.

fm.Upperside: Brownish gray blue
fm.Underside: Lacks cell end bars. On HW, Orange spot with inner white border

desc: A dark and distinctive Skipper

fm.Upperside: Dark, with translucent gold band in FW. Not as angular as Silver-spotted.
fm.Underside: Pale margin to hindwing is bold.
fm.Habitat: Open areas near woods, nectar flowers.

desc: Very Similar to Pearl Crecent

fm.Upperside: Males have more open orange in HW, females darker
fm.Underside: Very similar to Pearl, possibly less patterned.

desc: A stunning, uniquely patternred butterfly.

fm.Upperside: Dark interior with firey yellow-orange band on outer edge of wing.
fm.Underside: Similar to Mourning Cloak.

desc: An unique and unmistakable butterfly.

fm.Upperside: Triangular shaped white wings with black striping.
fm.Underside: Below similar but red band visible.

desc: One iNaturalist observation in June.  Seen elsewhere in the state in Summer and Fall.

fm.Upperside: Male Orange on either side of stigma. Female HW spots small in center (unlike Pecks).
fm.Underside: Orange brown with yellow spotting and yellow cell spot.

desc: An unexpected Hairstreak with lots of namesake lines in underwing.

fm.Upperside: Grayish-blue with one orange spot in HW.
fm.Underside: Many stripes on FW and HW, orange post median spots.
fm.Habitat: Thickets, woodlands and their edge habitat.
